Were You Asked This?
Design an ANN index system

Design a system that can efficiently retrieve the IDs of the most similar ~50 vectors to a given query vector from a dataset of ~10 billion vectors, supporting high-throughput read and write operations with approximate nearest neighbor search capabilities.

Asked at:

Google

A vector similarity search service is like the engine behind semantic search in apps such as image search, product recommendations, or retrieval-augmented generation. Given a query vector, it returns the IDs of the most similar vectors from a massive dataset using approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) techniques for low-latency results.

Interviewers ask this to see whether you can scale search across 10B vectors while balancing latency, recall, cost, and index freshness under continuous updates. They’re testing your ability to choose and justify ANN indexes, partition data, handle online overwrites, and manage query fanout/merge, all with clear SLAs and trade-offs. Expect to discuss memory footprints, compression, replication, and how you keep the index fresh without pausing the world.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to retrieve the top ~50 most similar vector IDs for a given query vector with low latency and tunable accuracy (recall).

Users should be able to overwrite an existing vector by its ID and have the change reflected in search results within an agreed freshness SLA.

Users should be able to handle sustained high-throughput workloads (~10K QPS reads and ~10K QPS writes) without significant degradation.

Users should be able to specify search parameters (e.g., distance metric, recall/latency trade-offs) per request or per index.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

What index structures and data layout let you serve 10B vectors with low latency and acceptable recall?

How will you handle continuous overwrites without pausing queries or doing full index rebuilds?

How do you partition, route, and merge results to avoid full-cluster fanout?

How will you meet SLOs for p95/p99 latency under failures and skew while controlling cost?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Reads

10K QPS queries against 10B vectors require aggressive read scaling via sharding, bounded fanout, and hierarchical indexes to avoid cluster-wide broadcasts and to keep p95 latency low.

Read More
Scaling Writes

Continuous overwrites at ~10K QPS need a durable ingestion path, batched index updates, and compaction strategies (base + delta indexes) to maintain throughput without blocking queries.

Read More
Real-time Updates

The system must reflect overwrites quickly. A change-log-driven pipeline with near-real-time index refresh, versioned swaps, and per-ID visibility guarantees is central to meeting freshness SLAs.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides a durable, high-throughput write log for vector overwrites, enabling decoupled ingestion, backpressure handling, and coordinated index rebuilds and delta updates across shards.

Read More
Cassandra

Cassandra scales horizontally for storing the source of truth: per-ID vectors, versions, and metadata. It supports high write rates, TTL/version semantics, and multi-DC replication for durability.

Read More
Elasticsearch

Elasticsearch offers built-in kNN (e.g., HNSW) and sharding/replication machinery. While a custom FAISS/ScaNN layer is common in industry, Elasticsearch can anchor the discussion around ANN indexing plus distributed query, routing, and cluster operations.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

FB Post Search

Both require building and maintaining a large distributed index, routing queries to relevant shards, and merging partial results with tight latency budgets and freshness constraints.

Read
Practice
Web Crawler

The crawler’s indexing pipeline and periodic reindex/compaction are analogous to maintaining a base ANN index plus deltas, handling continuous content changes and versioned index swaps.

Read
Practice
Distributed Cache

Hotset management, sharding, replication, and tail-latency mitigation in a distributed cache map closely to caching hot centroids/vectors, avoiding full-cluster fanout, and ensuring predictable latency.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Scanning all vectors or proposing exact kNN over 10B items

A full scan or exact kNN is infeasible at 10B scale for 10K QPS; it ignores the need for hierarchical indexing, compression, and sharding, leading to catastrophic latency and cost.

Single monolithic index with synchronous updates

Requiring in-place updates on a huge index causes lock contention, long pauses, or downtime. Without a base+delta strategy and versioned swaps, you can’t meet freshness and availability goals.

Ignoring memory footprint and compression trade-offs

Storing raw FP32 vectors and heavy graph structures entirely in RAM is unrealistic at 10B scale. Without PQ/OPQ or tiered storage, the design will blow through memory and budget and won’t be operable.

Question Timeline

See when this question was last asked and where, including any notes left by other candidates.

Company
Company
Level
​
Level
Region
All Regions
Region

Early October, 2025

Google

Senior Manager

Design a system for retrieving the IDs of the most similar ~50 vectors (by e.g. Euclidean distance) to a given query vector from a dataset of ~10B (~10^10) vectors.  Each vector is an ordered list of 100 floats and has a corresponding unique ID.  The dataset receives ~10K requests / second to overwrite a vector with a new one and ~10K query (read) requests per second.  Vectors are never inserted or deleted, only overwritten (i.e. IDs are never inserted or removed).
